implication: Hash-table inference: a known premise was matched in memory, producing this conclusion as a new valid expression.
expansion: A named expression was expanded into its compiled definition structure from the GL binary.
disintegration: A compound expression (AND or existence) was decomposed into its constituent parts.
task formulation: The premise of the theorem under proof — the root assumption from which the derivation starts.
equality1: Argument substitution via equality: if a=b is known and P(a) holds, then P(b) follows.
equality2: Transitivity of equality: from a=b and b=c, conclude a=c.
recursion: Introduction of the induction hypothesis. In the base case: equality to the initial value. In the induction step: the successor step assumption.
theorem: A previously proven theorem used as an inference rule in this derivation.
reformulation for integration and: Reformulation for integration of an AND-category expression. The elements are combined back into their compound AND form.
reformulation for integration >[bound]: Reformulation for integration of an existence expression. The bound variable inside >[...] was determined by the preceding integration process and must be kept — it is not freely chosen.
reformulation for integration >[]: Reformulation for integration of an existence expression. The bound variable has been consumed by the integration process, so the quantifier >[...] is empty.
expansion for integration: Expression expanded in preparation for reverse-disintegration (integration). The target expression is expanded to expose its internal structure.
premise element: A premise of an implication used as input during the integration (reverse-disintegration) process.
validity name: Ties an expression to its implication scope during integration. Ensures expressions are matched within the correct validity context.
anchor handling: Anchor variable substitution: a variable in the anchor expression is replaced with a concrete value from the domain.
mirrored from: This theorem is the mirror (left-right swap of output variable) of another proved theorem.
reformulated from: This theorem is a reformulation of another proved theorem, expressing the same fact in a different structural form.
necessity for equality (hypo): An equality introduced as a hypothesis to collapse duplicate variables into a single representative.
externally provided theorem: A theorem injected from an external file, not proved by GL in this run. Accepted as given.
incubator back reformulation: An operator-equality theorem back-reformulated to direct operator form. Converts (=[op(a,b), c]) into (op[a,b,c]). NOT a valid mathematical step. Incubator output is never used in the main proof path — the incubator is a heuristic tool to optimize runtime (counterexample filtering). A real full-provenance calculator will not use this operation.
equalize variable: A variable equalization step within a multiplied proof. Multiple bound variables are set equal to reduce the search space.
multiplied from: This theorem was produced by partition-based variable equalization. A parent implication was split into copies where subsets of bound variables are identified.
contradiction: Expression proved by contradiction: both the expression and its negation were derived, confirming the negated form.
origin: Provenance tracking for proof chains. Records where an expression was derived from to maintain full auditability.
